name: Joseph of Arimathea
description: The British legends of Joseph of Arimathea center on the belief that he traveled to Britain, specifically Glastonbury, bringing with him either the Holy Grail (the cup used at the Last Supper) or the Ark of the Covenant, or both. These traditions lack biblical support, with Joseph only mentioned in the Gospels as the man who donated his tomb for Jesus' burial. The British narrative emerged gradually, gaining prominence in the late medieval period and solidifying during the Tudor era. Proponents often cite the Glastonbury Thorn, said to have sprouted from Joseph's staff, as evidence, along with supposed links to Druidic traditions and ancient trade routes. The legend is deeply intertwined with Arthurian lore, with some versions claiming Joseph was related to Arthur's family. Mainstream scholarship views these claims as pious legends developed over time to enhance the religious significance of Glastonbury and legitimize royal claims by linking them to biblical history. The story evolved over centuries, influenced by political and religious agendas, and lacks verifiable historical evidence outside of Christian scripture. The enduring appeal of the Joseph of Arimathea narrative lies in its ability to connect British identity with Christian origins and the allure of sacred relics.
subtitle: Glastonbury, Grail, and British Christianity

claims.claim: Joseph of Arimathea brought the Holy Grail to Glastonbury.
claims.evidence: The Morte d'Arthur, Glastonbury Abbey traditions, the Chalice Well
claims.criticism: No biblical or early historical evidence supports this claim. The Grail legend is a later medieval invention.

claims.claim: Joseph of Arimathea planted the Glastonbury Thorn.
claims.evidence: The Glastonbury Thorn itself, local traditions linking it to Joseph's staff
claims.criticism: While the thorn exists, its connection to Joseph is purely legendary and lacks historical confirmation. Thorn trees are common in the region.

claims.claim: Joseph was a tin merchant who traded with Britain before Jesus's crucifixion.
claims.evidence: Speculation based on Joseph's wealth and the presence of tin mines in Cornwall
claims.criticism: There's no direct evidence to support this. It's based on assumptions about Joseph's profession and trade routes.

keyFigures.name: William of Malmesbury
keyFigures.role: Early chronicler who first mentions Glastonbury's association with early Christianity.

keyFigures.name: John Dee
keyFigures.role: Elizabethan scholar who promoted the legend of Joseph's arrival in Britain.

keyFigures.name: E. Raymond Capt
keyFigures.role: A proponent of the British Israelism movement that heavily features Joseph of Arimathea.

keyFigures.name: Ronald Hutton
keyFigures.role: Historian who analyzes the development of pagan and Christian traditions in Britain, including the Glastonbury legends.

timeline.event: William of Malmesbury mentions Glastonbury's early Christian origins.
Thomas Malory's *Le Morte d'Arthur* popularizes the Grail legend's connection to Britain.
John Dee promotes the idea of Joseph's arrival in Britain.
The legends of Joseph of Arimathea are further developed by esoteric and British Israelist movements.

sources.author: Ronald Hutton
sources.work: Pagan Religions of the Ancient British Isles
sources.title: Ronald Hutton

sources.author: Richard Barber
sources.work: The Holy Grail: Imagination Within Vision
sources.title: Richard Barber
